topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too harsh, exposing every brushstroke of 'Nocturne in Sepia' with unflattering scrutiny. I stood near the entrance, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white islands in the gloom. A large, glossy placard, bearing my name and the title, sat beside the canvas, mocking me with its official air. I took a tentative step forward, my gaze darting from the vibrant, swirling depths of the painting to the faces of the patrons—a polite, distant appraisal that offered no warmth, no acknowledgment of the struggle it took to create those shadows. I cleared my throat, the sound thin in the hushed room. "Excuse me," I managed, my voice cracking slightly. "Who authorized this display?" The silence that followed was heavy, thick enough to choke on.

[story 2]

I found myself standing in the center of the room, my feet rooted to the polished floor. The air tasted stale, thick with the scent of expensive perfume and old varnish. My eyes traced the lines of the sculpture—my sculpture—a piece I had labored over for three years, now resting on a pedestal as if it belonged to someone else. A strange, cold pressure settled in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I walked slowly around the perimeter, my fingers brushing against the cool metal of the frame. When a woman with an air of practiced nonchalance approached, smiling vaguely, I felt a sudden urge to retreat. "This isn't mine," I stated, the words coming out sharper than I intended, laced with a tremor I couldn't suppress. Her smile faltered, a brief flicker of something unreadable in her eyes before she offered a dismissive wave of her hand.

[story 3]

The sheer audacity of it made my stomach clench. I watched from across the crowded room as people moved around the exhibited pieces, their murmurs sounding like a low, constant hum. I felt a profound disconnect, as if I were observing a foreign, intricate mechanism. I approached the display cautiously, my posture stiff, every muscle coiled tight. When I reached the painting—my most personal work—I stopped just short of touching it. My breath hitched. "I need to speak to someone about this," I said, my tone carefully neutral, trying to keep the tremor from shaking my entire frame. A guard, looking profoundly bored, approached. He glanced at the artwork, then at me, his expression unreadable, before he simply nodded and walked away, leaving me alone with the silent, undeniable presence of my creation.
